title: Ezekiel 36
subtitle: Prophecy to the Mountains of Israel

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — New Heart Promised

sections.header: Verses 1-15 — The Mountains of Israel Restored

heb.paragraph: The direct address to the mountains personalizes the land as a participant in redemption. In chapter 6, Ezekiel was commanded to prophesy against the mountains in judgment; now in chapter 36 the same mountains receive a message of restoration. The reversal is deliberate and structural: what was cursed is now blessed.

The noun shemamah appears repeatedly in the judgment oracles. The mountains became a shemamah because of Israel's sins, but now God promises to reverse the desolation. The same word echoes in chapter 35 describing Edom's fate: Edom's desolation is permanent, but Israel's is temporary. The contrast is theologically freighted.

panels.ctx: This oracle mirrors and reverses chapter 35 in a deliberate literary pairing. Chapter 35 addressed Mount Seir (Edom) with judgment; chapter 36 addresses the mountains of Israel with restoration. The contrast is geographic, theological, and structural. The nations (v. 3) have mocked Israel's desolate mountains, claiming them as plunder. God's response is fierce: the mountains will produce fruit and bear people again (vv. 8-12). The land is not merely scenery; in Israelite theology, the land is covenantal real estate, promised to Abraham and inseparable from God's faithfulness. Restoring the mountains is restoring the covenant.

cross.note: The judgment oracle against the mountains of Israel that chapter 36 deliberately reverses. In chapter 6, the mountains were told to hear the word of destruction; in chapter 36, they hear the word of restoration.
The Levitical promise that God would remember the covenant with the land itself. Ezekiel's mountains oracle fulfills this covenantal memory.
Isaiah's parallel promise that the desolate land will become too small for the returning population, echoing Ezekiel's vision of multiplied people.

notes.note: God addresses the mountains because the land is central to His covenant promises. The Abrahamic covenant included land as an irrevocable gift (Gen 15:18-21). The nations' mockery of desolate Israel insults God's promise, and He takes it personally. The jealousy of verse 5 is covenantal jealousy, the same burning commitment that drives all of redemptive history.

The fruitfulness promised to the mountains anticipates a literal, physical restoration of the people to the land. The trees bearing fruit and the cities being rebuilt are not merely metaphors for spiritual blessing but concrete, geographic fulfillment. Israel's future in the land is as certain as the character of God who promised it.

The personification of the land as one that 'devours' its inhabitants echoes the spies' report in Numbers 13:32. God promises to remove that stigma permanently. The land will no longer be an instrument of judgment but of blessing.

The prophet speaks to the mountains because God's promises are tied to specific places and peoples. Calvin sees this as God's providential ordering of geography within redemptive history. The nations' contempt for Israel's land is ultimately contempt for God's election, which cannot be undone by political catastrophe.

Calvin notes that God often reverses the most extreme desolation to demonstrate that restoration is entirely His work, not human effort. The mountains producing fruit after devastation mirrors the pattern of death and resurrection that runs through all of Scripture.

The phrase 'ancient heights' (bamot olam) echoes the cultic high places that were sites of idolatry. The irony is pointed: the very heights where Israel sinned are now claimed by pagan nations, completing the reversal of Israel's fortunes.

The phrase 'in the fire of my jealousy' uses qin'ah, covenantal jealousy. This is not petty emotion but the fierce, protective commitment of a husband for his wife, a standard prophetic metaphor for God's relationship with Israel.

Block observes that this oracle functions as the positive counterpart to the Edom oracle (ch. 35), creating an envelope structure: Edom judged / Israel restored. The address to the mountains is a prophetic convention (cf. Mic 6:1-2) that calls the landscape itself as witness to God's covenantal actions. The recognition formula in verse 11 extends to the land: God's identity is revealed not only through human history but through the physical transformation of geography.

Zimmerli identifies this oracle as a Heilsorakel (salvation oracle) structured as a direct reversal of the judgment oracle in 6:1-7. The form-critical parallel is exact: same addressee (mountains), same opening formula, opposite content. This structural reversal is a hallmark of Ezekiel's literary architecture, where the prophet deliberately echoes earlier judgment speeches in later restoration speeches, creating a literary theology of reversal.

heb.paragraph: Ezekiel the priest uses the most potent purity category available: menstrual uncleanness (cf. Lev 15:19-30). The comparison is deliberately shocking. Israel's conduct made the land ritually contaminated in the same way that menstrual blood renders sacred space inaccessible. This is priestly logic operating at full intensity: sin is not merely moral failure but ritual contamination that makes God's presence impossible.

panels.ctx: Before describing restoration, Ezekiel explains why exile happened. The logic is priestly: Israel defiled the land with bloodshed and idolatry (v. 18), so God scattered them among the nations (v. 19). But exile created a new problem: among the nations, Israel 'profaned my holy name' (v. 20) because the nations concluded that Israel's God was too weak to protect His people. The exile thus created a theological crisis: God's reputation was at stake. This sets up the crucial motivation for restoration in the next section.

cross.note: The land itself 'vomits out' its inhabitants when defiled. Ezekiel's priestly vocabulary draws directly on this Levitical theology of land-contamination.
Isaiah makes the same point: among the nations, God's name is 'constantly blasphemed' because of Israel's condition. Both prophets identify the exile as a crisis of divine reputation.

notes.note: The comparison to menstrual uncleanness is not about women but about ritual contamination. Israel's violence and idolatry rendered the land as unfit for God's presence as a ritually impure person was unfit for the tabernacle. The exile was God's response to unbearable contamination of His holy land.

The profanation of God's name among the nations is the theological hinge of this chapter. God's motivation for restoration is not Israel's merit but His own name. This is the most God-centered passage in the prophets: salvation serves God's glory before it serves human need.

Calvin emphasizes that God compares Israel's conduct to uncleanness not to demean but to expose the gravity of sin. The exile was not arbitrary punishment but a necessary response to intolerable defilement. Calvin draws the application: every age of the church must reckon with the possibility that God will withdraw His presence from communities that defile His name.

The Hebrew niddah refers specifically to menstrual impurity (Lev 15:19-30). The NET translators note this is one of the strongest purity metaphors available to a priestly author. It communicates total ritual incompatibility between the people's conduct and God's holy presence.

Block notes that this retrospective functions as a theological explanation for exile that avoids both fatalism and sentimentality. The exile was deserved (Israel defiled the land), but God's concern for His name creates the basis for undeserved restoration. Block identifies this as 'grace through divine self-interest' — God acts for His name, but the beneficiaries are the people.

Zimmerli identifies the niddah comparison as uniquely Ezekielian: no other prophet uses this specific purity category for Israel's corporate sin. It reflects Ezekiel's priestly formation, where holiness categories structure all theological reasoning. The dispersion-as-purging motif connects to Ezekiel's broader theology that exile is both punishment and purification.

sections.header: Verses 22-32 — The New Heart and New Spirit

heb.paragraph: The lev (heart) in Hebrew is not the seat of emotion but the center of will, thought, and decision-making. A 'new heart' means a fundamentally reconstituted inner person: new desires, new loyalties, new capacity for obedience. This is not reformation but re-creation. The adjective chadash (new) suggests something qualitatively different, not merely repaired.

The ruach is paired with lev but goes deeper: it is the animating principle, the breath of life itself. God's own ruach will be placed within them (v. 27), making obedience not an external demand but an internal compulsion. This promise anticipates Pentecost and the indwelling Spirit of the New Testament. The old spirit of rebellion is replaced with God's own animating presence.

Stone is impervious, unresponsive, dead. The heart of stone cannot receive instruction, cannot feel conviction, cannot obey. Replacing stone with flesh (basar) means replacing imperviousness with sensitivity, death with life. This is the most vivid image of regeneration in the Hebrew Bible.

panels.ctx: This passage is one of the most theologically significant in the entire Old Testament. The new heart and new spirit promise (vv. 26-27) parallels Jeremiah's new covenant (Jer 31:31-34) but goes further: Ezekiel does not merely promise internal law-writing but the implantation of God's own Spirit. The motivation is stated bluntly: 'It is not for your sake, people of Israel, that I am going to do these things, but for the sake of my holy name' (v. 22). Restoration is entirely grace, entirely God-initiated, entirely for God's glory. The sequence is precise: cleansing (v. 25), new heart (v. 26), Spirit implantation (v. 27), obedience (v. 27b), land restoration (v. 28). The order is theologically non-negotiable: transformation precedes obedience, not the reverse.

cross.note: Jeremiah's new covenant promises internal law-writing and universal knowledge of God. Ezekiel's new heart/spirit promise is the parallel formulation: both prophets envision transformation from within rather than external command.

Joel's promise of the Spirit poured out on all flesh extends Ezekiel's new-spirit promise to its universal scope. Peter cites Joel at Pentecost (Acts 2:16-21) as the fulfillment of this prophetic trajectory.

Jesus' teaching on being 'born of water and the Spirit' likely alludes to Ezekiel 36:25-27, where water cleansing and Spirit implantation are paired. Jesus expects Nicodemus, 'a teacher of Israel,' to know this passage.
Paul's theology of the indwelling Spirit fulfills Ezekiel's promise: God's Spirit dwells within believers, producing the obedience that the law demanded but could not generate.

notes.note: This is one of the most God-centered statements in all of Scripture. Restoration is not motivated by Israel's repentance, worthiness, or prayers, but solely by God's concern for His own holy name. This shatters any notion of merit-based salvation. God saves for His glory, and the beneficiaries are chosen sinners who deserve nothing.

The sequence is soteriological: cleansing from sin (justification), a new heart (regeneration), the indwelling Spirit (sanctification), and resultant obedience (the fruit of salvation). This is the Old Testament's clearest anticipation of the New Covenant's saving work. The water of verse 25 is not baptism but the ceremonial sprinkling of purification (cf. Num 19:17-19).

The restoration to the land and agricultural abundance fulfill the Abrahamic and Mosaic covenant blessings (Deut 28:1-14). This will find its ultimate realization in the millennial kingdom, when Israel dwells in the land under the New Covenant.

True repentance follows regeneration, not the reverse. Israel will loathe their past sins only after God has transformed their hearts. This demolishes the idea that repentance earns salvation; rather, salvation produces repentance.

Calvin finds here the purest expression of sola gratia: God acts for His name, not for human merit. The glory of God is the first cause of salvation, and human benefit is the secondary effect. Calvin applies this to counter any theology that grounds salvation in human initiative or cooperation.

Calvin identifies the water as symbolic purification and the new heart as regeneration by the Holy Spirit. He stresses that the heart of stone is the natural human condition and that only sovereign divine action can replace it. No human effort can soften stone; only the Creator who made the heart can remake it.

The 'clean water' sprinkled refers to the ritual purification ceremony of Numbers 19, not to baptism. The NET translators note that the Hebrew verb zaraq (sprinkle) is technical priestly vocabulary for ritual cleansing with specially prepared water.

The phrase 'I will put my Spirit in you' uses the verb natan (give/place) rather than a verb of pouring. The emphasis is on God's initiative and sovereignty in placing His Spirit within the people, not on any human receptivity or preparation.

Block identifies this as the theological center of the entire book. The pattern is entirely theocentric: God acts for His name (v. 22), God cleanses (v. 25), God gives a new heart (v. 26), God places His Spirit (v. 27). The fourfold divine initiative leaves no room for human contribution. Block notes the deliberate echo of the creation narrative: God formed Adam from clay and breathed life into him; now God re-forms Israel with a new heart and breathes His Spirit into them. This is nothing less than a new creation.

Zimmerli designates this passage as the 'centre of the message of the book' and locates it within the form-critical category of divine self-disclosure speech. The motivation clause 'for my holy name's sake' is not mere self-interest but the theological principle that God's character is the ground of all historical action. Zimmerli sees the new heart/spirit promise as Ezekiel's unique contribution to the prophetic tradition: where Jeremiah speaks of internalized torah, Ezekiel speaks of implanted ruach — a more radical transformation that goes beyond knowledge to power.

sections.header: Verses 33-38 — Rebuilt Cities and Multiplied People

heb.gloss: like the flock of Eden / like the flock at festivals
heb.paragraph: The comparison to a flock evokes both Eden (paradise restored) and the festival flocks that filled Jerusalem at Passover, Weeks, and Tabernacles. The cities will be as full of people as Jerusalem was full of sacrificial animals during the great pilgrim feasts. The image combines agricultural abundance with cultic celebration.

panels.ctx: The chapter concludes with a vision of repopulation and rebuilding that matches the covenantal blessings of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28. The desolate cities will be rebuilt and inhabited; the people will multiply like flocks. The recognition formula appears again (v. 38): 'Then they will know that I am the LORD.' This formula has appeared over 70 times in Ezekiel, and here it reaches its positive fulfillment: God is known not only through judgment but through restoration.

cross.note: God's covenant promise to make Israel fruitful, multiply them, and keep His covenant with them. Ezekiel 36:33-38 is the prophetic reaffirmation of these Levitical promises after exile.
Isaiah's parallel vision of the barren woman singing for joy because her children will be more numerous than before. Both prophets see post-exile restoration as exceeding the pre-exile state.

notes.note: The rebuilt cities and multiplied population point toward the millennial kingdom, when Israel will inhabit the land in unprecedented numbers under the New Covenant. The comparison to festival flocks anticipates the temple worship described in chapters 40-48. God's promise to be 'inquired of' by Israel (v. 37) means that He will once again answer prayer and act on behalf of His restored people.

Calvin sees the rebuilt cities as God's demonstration that He alone can restore what He has destroyed. Human effort cannot rebuild what divine judgment has leveled. The multiplication of people like flocks indicates God's sovereign control over population and prosperity.

The verb darash (to seek, inquire of) in the niphal (passive/reflexive) means 'to allow oneself to be consulted.' God commits to being accessible to Israel again, reversing the prophetic silence that characterizes exile.

Block notes the inclusio formed with 36:1-15: the chapter opens with mountains addressed and closes with cities filled. The geographic scope moves from landscape to settlement, from terrain to community. The flock metaphor in verse 38 connects backward to the shepherd oracle of chapter 34 and forward to the dry bones of chapter 37.

Zimmerli observes that the concluding recognition formula in verse 38 transforms the formula from a judgment marker to a salvation marker. Throughout the judgment oracles, 'they will know that I am the LORD' accompanied destruction; here it accompanies rebuilding. The form is identical but the content is inverted.

themes.note: Sovereignty reaches 10: the entire chapter is driven by God's initiative for His name's sake. Covenant and Holiness are both high because the new heart promise is the ultimate expression of covenant renewal and the restoration of holiness. Mercy rises to 8 as undeserved grace dominates. Mission scores 7 because God's restoration has a witness function: the nations will know.

lit.note: The chapter moves from external restoration (land, vv. 1-15) through theological explanation (why exile happened, vv. 16-21) to internal transformation (new heart/spirit, vv. 22-32) and culminates in communal renewal (cities and people, vv. 33-38). The structural center is the new heart passage, which is also the theological center of the book.

ppl.text: Before describing restoration, Ezekiel explains why exile happened…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Prophecy to the Mountains of Israel

translations.text: “ ‘But you, mountains of Israel, will produce branches and fruit for my people Israel, for they will soon come home.
“But you, O mountains of Israel, shall shoot forth your branches and yield your fruit to my people Israel, for they will soon come home.

therefore, mountains of Israel, hear the word of the Sovereign Lord: This is what the Sovereign Lord says to the mountains and hills, to the ravines and valleys, to the desolate ruins and the deserted towns that have been plundered and ridiculed by the rest of the nations around you—

therefore, O mountains of Israel, hear the word of the Lord GOD: Thus says the Lord GOD to the mountains and the hills, the ravines and the valleys, the desolate wastes and the deserted cities, which have become a prey and derision to the rest of the nations all around,

debate: The Hebrew niddah refers specifically to menstrual impurity (Lev 15:19-30). The NET translators note this is one of the strongest purity metaphors ava…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Prophecy to the Mountains of Israel within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.anchor: Prophecy to the Mountains of Israel
